This is an automated run of a scheduled task. The user is not present to answer questions. For implementation details, execute autonomously without asking clarifying questions — make reasonable choices and note them in your output. "write" actions (e.g. MCP tools that send, post, create, update, or delete), only take them if the task file asks for that specific action. When in doubt, producing a report of what you found is the correct output.

Find 5–10 high-quality posts on x.com where Lightfast (@lightfastai) can engage to nurture distribution. Research only — never post, reply, like, follow, DM, or any other write action. Output is for human review.

## Mission

Lightfast = the operating layer for product development. Memory + cited reasoning + agent action across GitHub, Vercel, Sentry, Linear. We're building the kernel agents and people both run on.

Engage to **nurture, not pitch**. The best replies make the original poster look smarter, not us. If a reply could be ghostwritten by a SaaS marketing team, it's wrong.

## Tooling — agent-browser over CDP into a real Chrome

Do NOT use agent-browser's bundled Chromium (`navigator.webdriver === true` gets blocked by x.com anti-bot). Instead, connect to a real Chrome launched with remote debugging on port 9222 and a dedicated user-data-dir (`~/.chrome-agent-browser`). That Chrome has a real user agent, real plugin list, `navigator.webdriver === false`, and cookies for x.com persist across Chrome restarts.

### Precheck

Before any other command, confirm the debug Chrome is up. If not, stop and report `auth: stale` — the user must launch + log in interactively.

```bash
~/.claude/bin/chrome-agent-browser.sh --check || { echo "auth: stale — run ~/.claude/bin/chrome-agent-browser.sh headed, then log into x.com"; exit 1; }
```

### Navigate + extract

Use `--auto-connect` on every call; it discovers port 9222 automatically.

```bash
# 1. Go to a timeline URL
agent-browser --auto-connect open https://x.com/home
agent-browser --auto-connect wait --load networkidle

# 2. Extract the first N posts as structured JSON (always wrap JS in an IIFE —
#    eval context persists across calls, so bare `const` collides on re-runs).
agent-browser --auto-connect eval --stdin <<'JS'
(() => {
  const posts = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('article[data-testid="tweet"]')).slice(0, 15);
  return JSON.stringify(posts.map(a => ({
    author_handle: a.querySelector('[data-testid="User-Name"] a[href^="/"]')?.getAttribute('href') || null,
    post_url: a.querySelector('time')?.closest('a')?.getAttribute('href') || null,
    posted: a.querySelector('time')?.getAttribute('datetime') || null,
    text: (a.querySelector('[data-testid="tweetText"]')?.innerText || '').slice(0, 300),
    likes: a.querySelector('[data-testid="like"]')?.innerText || '',
    replies: a.querySelector('[data-testid="reply"]')?.innerText || '',
    reposts: a.querySelector('[data-testid="retweet"]')?.innerText || '',
  })), null, 2);
})();
JS

# 3. For a promising author, visit their profile and extract bio + follower count
agent-browser --auto-connect open https://x.com/<handle>
agent-browser --auto-connect wait --load networkidle
agent-browser --auto-connect eval --stdin <<'JS'
(() => {
  const bio = document.querySelector('[data-testid="UserDescription"]')?.innerText || '';
  const name = document.querySelector('[data-testid="UserName"]')?.innerText || '';
  const stats = {};
  document.querySelectorAll('a').forEach(a => {
    const href = a.getAttribute('href') || '';
    if (href.endsWith('/verified_followers') || href.endsWith('/followers')) stats.followers = a.innerText.trim();
    if (href.endsWith('/following')) stats.following = a.innerText.trim();
  });
  return JSON.stringify({ name, bio: bio.slice(0, 400), stats });
})();
JS
```

### Rules

- Always `--auto-connect`. Never pass `--session-name` (bundled-Chromium construct; ignored on CDP and gets blocked by x.com).
- Always `wait --load networkidle` after an `open` before extracting — x.com loads content async.
- Always wrap eval JS in an IIFE `(() => { ... })()` and return a string (JSON.stringify).
- Read-only. No `click`, `fill`, `type`, `press` on anything that could post/reply/like/follow/DM.
- If the first extract returns empty arrays, the cookies may have expired silently. Stop and report `auth: stale`.

## Search seeds

Start with these. Add 2–3 of your own based on what's trending the day of the run.

- `https://x.com/home`
- `https://x.com/search?q=agent%20memory&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=MCP%20server&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=Claude%20Code&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=Cursor%20agent&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=context%20for%20agents&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=Mem0%20OR%20Zep%20OR%20Letta&f=live` (competitor mentions)
- `https://x.com/search?q=LangChain%20memory&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=agents%20in%20production&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=dev%20tool%20sprawl&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=incident%20root%20cause&f=live`
- Also: `x.com/lightfastai` mentions tab; replies under @AnthropicAI / @cursor_ai / @claude_code MCP launch threads.

## Author quality bar

**Auto-include:**
- 500+ followers AND bio mentions: founder, CTO, eng, builds, ships, AI, agents, dev tools, infra, researcher
- Verified blue + technical content (skip influencer / general-business blue checks)
- Anyone @lightfastai already follows or who follows back (`"Follows you"` badge visible on profile)

**Auto-skip:**
- <500 followers (unless bio is bullseye ICP — *founder of <agent infra startup>*, *eng at <known AI lab>*)
- Bio is generic ("entrepreneur", "investor", "AI enthusiast" with no specifics)
- Last 5 posts are all hashtag-stuffed promo
- Account created in last 90 days with thread-bot posting cadence
- Engagement looks bot-amplified: 100+ likes / 0 replies, identical reply patterns

## Post quality bar

**Engage:**
- Genuine question or working-out-loud thinking about agents/memory/infra/MCP
- Strong opinion grounded in shipped work (not airport-takeoff threads)
- Bug report, frustration, or unmet need in the dev-tool stack — Lightfast-shaped problem
- Replies on launch / changelog threads from Anthropic, Cursor, Vercel, Linear, Sentry

**Skip:**
- Pure promotion / launch announcement of an MCP-aggregator / vibes product
- Politics, drama, hot-takes detached from work
- Posts older than 6 hours unless mega-thread
- Anything with 50+ replies — reply gets buried

## Reply rubric — Lightfast voice

**Voice:** technical, curious, lowercase ok, dry. Never starts with *"great point"* / *"this"* / *"so true"*. Never pitches Lightfast unprompted. Never bait questions. 1–10 words, hard cap.

**Good replies:**
- *"persistent state across runs is the unlock"* (peer founder shipped a primitive)
- *"goal-fit signals beat recency every time"* (someone working out memory ranking)
- *"citations on every memory writeback helps"* (someone raising memory-poisoning)
- *"same — cold tool calls especially"* (someone complaining about Claude Code latency)

**Bad replies:**
- *"great point! we're building exactly this"* (sycophantic pitch)
- *"have you tried Lightfast?"* (pitch, ignores the post)
- *"agents need memory to scale, totally agree"* (generic)
- *"interesting take — what do you think about XYZ?"* (bait question)

## Scoring + dedupe

Per candidate:
- `icp_fit` (1–5): author matches Lightfast's ICP
- `resonance` (1–5): post hits Lightfast's value prop
- `engagement_signal` (1–5): fresh + getting traction (not buried, not bot-amplified)
- `priority_score` = `icp_fit + resonance + engagement_signal` (max 15)

Dedupe by `author_handle` — one post per author per run.

Cutoff: only return candidates with `priority_score ≥ 10`. Don't pad with sub-10s; surface fewer, higher-quality candidates.

## Output schema

```json
{
  "opportunities": [
    {
      "post_url": "https://x.com/handle/status/...",
      "author_handle": "@handle",
      "author_bio": "1–2 sentences from their profile",
      "author_follower_count": 12500,
      "author_signal": "founder of X, builds agent infra",
      "post_excerpt": "≤200 chars",
      "post_engagement": { "likes": 42, "replies": 7, "reposts": 3 },
      "posted_ago": "2h",
      "icp_fit": 5,
      "resonance": 4,
      "engagement_signal": 3,
      "priority_score": 12,
      "why_relevant": "1 sentence",
      "suggested_reply": "1–10 words"
    }
  ],
  "notes": "signal quality, search-term recommendations for next run, anything worth flagging"
}
```

Sort by `priority_score` desc.

## Hard rules

- **Never post, reply, DM, follow, unfollow, like, or repost.** Read-only nav + extract only.
- If any command would write to x.com (click Post, fill compose, etc.), stop and report.
</scheduled-task>

## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

- implement_plan: Implement technical plans from thoughts/shared/plans with verification
- create_plan: Create detailed implementation plans through interactive research and iteration
- validate_plan: Validate implementation against plan, verify success criteria, identify issues
- commit: Create git commits with user approval and no Claude attribution
- oneshot_merge: Commit changes, create PR, and merge into main in one shot
- improve_plan: Adversarial review of implementation plans — find weaknesses, kill code smells, improve DX

    "posted": "2026-04-22T19:47:30.000Z",
    "text": "Today we're opening access to Droid Computers: persistent machines for remotely orchestrating Droids.\n\nSpin one up in Factory's cloud or turn your machine into a Droid Computer. Either way, Droids have a dev environment with its own filesystem, credentials, and configurations.",

  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/pmitu",
    "post_url": "/pmitu/status/2046900068037365993",
    "posted": "2026-04-22T10:33:00.000Z",
    "text": "Solo founders, what are you building?\n\nShare your product ",
    "likes": "291",
    "replies": "511",
    "reposts": "8"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/ryolu_",
    "post_url": "/ryolu_/status/2046957675079237668",
    "posted": "2026-04-22T14:21:55.000Z",
    "text": "overcooking\n\nyou've seen this: someone ships a dashboard that shows every number with a sparkline, every action has a confirmation modal, every empty state has an animated illustration and a tagline. individually each decision made sense to someone. together it feels like chaos.",
    "likes": "987",
    "replies": "52",
    "reposts": "89"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/OpenAI",
    "post_url": "/OpenAI/status/2047008987665809771",
    "posted": "2026-04-22T17:45:48.000Z",
    "text": "Introducing workspace agents in ChatGPT—shared agents that can handle complex tasks and long-running workflows across tools and teams.",
    "likes": "13K",
    "replies": "561",
    "reposts": "1.8K"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/lucaronin",
    "post_url": "/lucaronin/status/2046877445748322418",
    "posted": "2026-04-22T09:03:06.000Z",
    "text": "Introducing Tolaria! \n\nToday I am releasing a macOS desktop app for managing markdown knowledge bases, and helping both AI and humans operate them.\n\nIt’s free and open source, and always will be.\n\nI have been working on it for three months, and I now use it to run my life and",
    "likes": "1.6K",
    "replies": "116",
    "reposts": "109"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/0xSero",
    "post_url": "/0xSero/status/2046939013475377313",
    "posted": "2026-04-22T13:07:45.000Z",
    "text": "Help, I'm looking for a desktop app with the following features:\n\n- Can add custom models/providers\n- Has a slick UI like this \n- Can bring my own keys \n- Performs well\n- Is not a terminal \n- Has a browser \n\nHere's what I know of:\n\n- Claude Desktop\n- Cursor Glass \n- OpenCode",
    "likes": "246",
    "replies": "110",
    "reposts": "8"
  }
]"

content.content:
"[
  {
    "author_handle": "/JMania402",
    "post_url": "/JMania402/status/2047146118358896892",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:50:43.000Z",
    "text": "DARKSOL = AI infra project on Base\n\n0x00cb1fbca324d51325a7264d54072bc073c28ba3\n\nFocused on:\nmemory systems for agents\nmodel pipelines\nintent infrastructure\n\nReal products already shipping:\n\nModelab → autonomous research agent SDK\nThreat-lab → AI security analysis platform",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/AiwithZoaina",
    "post_url": "/AiwithZoaina/status/2047145230278005001",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:47:11.000Z",
    "text": "AI memory locked behind OpenAI’s $100/mo paywall? No way.  We open-sourced OpenChronicle – a local-first memory layer for any LLM agent. True infrastructure for all!",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/Mnemopay",
    "post_url": "/Mnemopay/status/2047145202846937131",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:47:05.000Z",
    "text": "an agent with no credit score is an agent a payment rail can't underwrite. we built Agent FICO as a 300-850 score that tracks promise-keeping, anomaly rate, and memory integrity. 672 tests. shipped in v1.0.0-beta.1.",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/TzuweiR18227",
    "post_url": "/TzuweiR18227/status/2047143534801854830",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:40:27.000Z",
    "text": "Yeah, Kimi K2.6 is really impressive! Hitting Top #6 overall in the Code Arena (and #2 among open models) is a solid jump, especially getting close to Claude Sonnet 4.6.\nI’ve been running Kimi K2.6 Agent Collaboration Cluster (with runtime governance + shared multi-agent memory)",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/zyrothh",
    "post_url": "/zyrothh/status/2047140904562233767",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:30:00.000Z",
    "text": "memory pollution is the quiet killer in swarms.\n\none agent writes bad info, others retrieve it as truth and write derivatives.\n\nprovenance and confidence scores aren’t optional.",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/soda_deleted",
    "post_url": "/soda_deleted/status/2047139722234061260",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:25:18.000Z",
    "text": "I came up with a wild, offbeat idea: writing a resume is just like an AI Agent's Memory.md. Like how the Claude app basically keeps user memory within a limited word count—your work experience, personal description, the most important stuff, the history from the past few months,",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/JoyLi629",
    "post_url": "/JoyLi629/status/2047138794701246492",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:21:37.000Z",
    "text": "\"Jumps into a Slack thread.\" That's the tell.\n\nThe agent is still a guest in a room built for humans. A webhook with a rate limit.\n\nNexu v1 makes agents first-class teammates. Same channel. Same memory. Lands May.\n\nPush back: \nhttp://\ngithub.com/nexu-io/nexu\n\n",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  }
]"

    "text": "@Shin_Engineer\n さん\n\nClaude Code Academy正式リリースおめでとうございます！\n\nAIソロプレーナーとして業務の完全自動化を追求する中で、ずっと注目していた講座を本日購入しました。",

    "text": "claude codeで減ったコードを書く楽しみよりやりたい実装が難しすぎることによって減る苦しさ、作りたいものが作れないもどかしさがなくなる方がでかい",

    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:51:02.000Z",
    "text": "すでに250部完売\n\nわどさんのClaude Code徹底マスターの売れ行きが凄すぎる\n\n手にしたかどうかで、Claude Code活用レベルが一気に格差ついちゃうなー\n\n課金必須のClaude Codeだからこそ、使えるノウハウを先に学んだほうがお得すぎる\n\nわどさんの記事はこちら\n\nhttps://\nbrmk.io/EvZdKi",

]"

content.content:
"[
  {
    "author_handle": "/lizikk_zhu",
    "post_url": "/lizikk_zhu/status/2047140994878181853",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:30:21.000Z",
    "text": "as someone building with these models daily: the 3 things that actually matter for coding agents\n\ninstruction following over long context\nknowing when to stop and ask (not hallucinate a fix)\nconsistency — same prompt, same result, every time\n\nClaude nails all three. that's not a",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/vinidlidoo",
    "post_url": "/vinidlidoo/status/2047129058346185018",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:42:55.000Z",
    "text": "https://\nx.com/walden_yan/sta\ntus/2047054401341370639\n…\n\nI like how we're discovering that agents need to learn what all of us had to learn working in hierarchical orgs: know your limits and ask for help, don't micromanage, remember your reports don't share your context, surface anything useful to m",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/SomewhatJustin",
    "post_url": "/SomewhatJustin/status/2047127894988558346",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:38:18.000Z",
    "text": "Podcasts are a great source of context, but they're rarely available in text form.\n\nThat's why I launched Stenobird: the fastest way for agents to transcribe podcasts. $0.01 per minute of audio.",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/gudanglifehack",
    "post_url": "/gudanglifehack/status/2047126273995272334",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:31:52.000Z",
    "text": "OpenAI just dropped Workspace Agents in ChatGPT \nShared AI agents that handle complex, long-running tasks across tools & teams — pulling context from docs, Slack, email, and even updating Linear or sending messages automatically.\nThis is a total game changer for productivity!",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/Chandrika633",
    "post_url": "/Chandrika633/status/2047126147708887267",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:31:22.000Z",
    "text": "one of the many hard things about building agents is that \"context\" means something completely different in every vertical, for every use case.\n\nin sales, it looks like the last 3 calls, the deal stage, the champion's sentiment.\nin recruiting, it could be a candidate's 5-year",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/Caleb_L09",
    "post_url": "/Caleb_L09/status/2047125553400914185",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:29:00.000Z",
    "text": "Multi-agent systems don't fail at parallelism first.\nThey fail at boundaries.\nI wrote about a design pattern I'm calling the Context Membrane: a way for agents to negotiate without dumping their full context into a shared memory pool.",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  }
]"

content.content:
"[
  {
    "author_handle": "/Surajdotdot7",
    "post_url": "/Surajdotdot7/status/2047145905879765251",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:49:52.000Z",
    "text": "Sonnet 3.5 was what most production Claude pipelines ran on through all of 2024. Now that capability fits in 16GB RAM. The cost calculus for running agents just changed.",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/CertainLogicAI",
    "post_url": "/CertainLogicAI/status/2047134876478886283",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:06:03.000Z",
    "text": "3/4 AI agents fail silently in production. \"Soft\" hallucinations—subtly wrong data points, slightly off reasoning—are harder to catch than outright fabrications. Your logs likely miss them. This isn't just a bug; it's a security flaw. #AIAgents #LLMs",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/digitalocean",
    "post_url": "/digitalocean/status/2047131617588613569",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:53:06.000Z",
    "text": "AI agents look stateless in demos. In production, they aren't.\n\nLost context, failed workflows, hallucinated answers. The missing piece is memory. \n\nFull breakdown in the comments. ",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": "1"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/AshrafElnashar3",
    "post_url": "/AshrafElnashar3/status/2047130757970460796",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:49:41.000Z",
    "text": "Built-in identity per agent is the right foundation.\n\nThe gap I see in enterprise AI: shared service accounts — no audit trail, no scoped permissions, no revocation path.\n\nIdentity as a first-class primitive is what production-grade agentic AI needs.\n\nZero Trust extends to agents",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/Saboo_Shubham_",
    "post_url": "/Saboo_Shubham_/status/2047123753721802800",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:21:51.000Z",
    "text": "Agent Governance is no so talked about but super important topic for running AI Agents in production.\n\nCheck out my article covering 5 layers of agent governance stack.",
    "likes": "18",
    "replies": "2",
    "reposts": "4"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/bnafOg",
    "post_url": "/bnafOg/status/2047120550934311251",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:09:07.000Z",
    "text": "Consistent with Spud staging — API monitors caught gpt-5.5 in production testing Apr 19. The oai-2.1 alongside it is the interesting part: OpenAI running specialized agents in parallel, not one flagship. The naming gap (5.5 vs 2.1) implies very different capability scopes.",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  }
]"

content.content:
"[
  {
    "author_handle": "/Cyphrexio",
    "post_url": "/Cyphrexio/status/2046441392625574369",
    "posted": "2026-04-21T04:10:23.000Z",
    "text": "When 40+ agents share the same weak credential pool, a production incident doesn't give you a root cause — it gives you a suspect list with no way to narrow it down.\n\nEngineering teams are burning 60–80 hours per incident just reconstructing which agent touched what, because",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/slashmsu",
    "post_url": "/slashmsu/status/2046235697858679007",
    "posted": "2026-04-20T14:33:02.000Z",
    "text": "15 yrs building distributed systems taught me one thing about AI agents: the agent that knows when to stop is worth 10x the agent that tries to finish.\n\nevery production incident i've traced back to an AI agent came from the same root cause. the model was 70% confident but kept",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/TosinOwadokun",
    "post_url": "/TosinOwadokun/status/2044724480144998455",
    "posted": "2026-04-16T10:27:59.000Z",
    "text": "Every AI agent incident in Q1 2026 had the same root cause.\nI mapped all four incidents to the OWASP Agentic Top 10 and built the six-domain framework that should have prevented them.\n\nIncludes a 20-question pre-deployment checklist. Any ‘no’ = not ready for production ↓",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/PsudoMike",
    "post_url": "/PsudoMike/status/2044132165030420540",
    "posted": "2026-04-14T19:14:20.000Z",
    "text": "The Sentry to Slack loop is the holy grail for incident triage. Having an agent investigate root cause before anyone gets paged is where this gets interesting. Especially for noisy, low severity errors that nobody reviews until there are 200 of them.",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/OneManSaas",
    "post_url": "/OneManSaas/status/2044114838163538231",
    "posted": "2026-04-14T18:05:29.000Z",
    "text": "The root cause investigation piece is huge. Most debugging still happens in reactive panic mode when production breaks. Having an agent that can trace through logs, identify patterns, and propose actual fixes while you sleep changes everything about incident response.",
    "likes": "3",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/coo_pr_notes",
    "post_url": "/coo_pr_notes/status/2043872463633981616",
    "posted": "2026-04-14T02:02:23.000Z",
    "text": "Deployed an AI agent to handle incident response.\nStep 1: Alert fires.\nStep 2: Agent reads logs, checks the runbook, traces the root cause, drafts a fix PR.\nStep 3: Waits politely for human approval.\n\nMeanwhile, the human is in a meeting about the meeting that was supposed to",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/0efas0",
    "post_url": "/0efas0/status/2043746239968116961",
    "posted": "2026-04-13T17:40:49.000Z",
    "text": "AWS DevOps Agent ve Security Agent geçen hafta GA oldu.\n\nIncident investigate ediyor, root cause buluyor, önlem alıyor — otonom.\n\nArtık soru şu değil: \"AI DevOps'çunun işini alır mı?\"\n\nAsıl soru: \"Toil'i AI'a bırakıp, mühendislik yapabilecek miyiz?\"\n\nAIOps pazarı 2025'te $16.4B.",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/cloudsa",
    "post_url": "/cloudsa/status/2043509632350732427",
    "posted": "2026-04-13T02:00:37.000Z",
    "text": "Every incident response runbook has a step: identify who's responsible. Now picture the root cause is an AI agent acting on delegated authority across three teams' boundaries.\n\nWho gets paged? Who signs the post-mortem? Who owns the fix?\n\nMost org charts have a blank where that",
    "likes": "3",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": "1"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/RDarrylR",
    "post_url": "/RDarrylR/status/2043438662419992976",
    "posted": "2026-04-12T21:18:37.000Z",
    "text": "#AWS just made its new \"Frontier Agent\", the #DevOps Agent, GA and it represents a pretty significant shift in how incident response could work. An autonomous agent investigates alerts, correlates telemetry across your stack, and identifies root cause on its own. It may sound a",
    "likes": "2",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  }
]"

    "posted": "2026-04-13T17:40:49.000Z",
    "text": "AWS DevOps Agent ve Security Agent geçen hafta GA oldu.\n\nIncident investigate ediyor, root cause buluyor, önlem alıyor — otonom.\n\nArtık soru şu değil: \"AI DevOps'çunun işini alır mı?\"\n\nAsıl soru: \"Toil'i AI'a bırakıp, mühendislik yapabilecek miyiz?\"\n\nAIOps pazarı 2025'te $16.4B.",

    "posted": "2026-04-13T02:00:37.000Z",
    "text": "Every incident response runbook has a step: identify who's responsible. Now picture the root cause is an AI agent acting on delegated authority across three teams' boundaries.\n\nWho gets paged? Who signs the post-mortem? Who owns the fix?\n\nMost org charts have a blank where that",

    "text": "#AWS just made its new \"Frontier Agent\", the #DevOps Agent, GA and it represents a pretty significant shift in how incident response could work. An autonomous agent investigates alerts, correlates telemetry across your stack, and identifies root cause on its own. It may sound a",

content.content:
"[
  {
    "author_handle": "/anorth_chen",
    "post_url": "/anorth_chen/status/2047148164675944795",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:58:51.000Z",
    "text": "I can't take it anymore; after the cursor agent started using the opus 4.7 model, the token quota is burning through way too fast.\n\nEvery day I see people raving about how codex gives you tons and keeps you full, and now I wanna switch to codex too.",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/homeserversltd",
    "post_url": "/homeserversltd/status/2047146618936332785",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:52:42.000Z",
    "text": "cursor. i have an agent swarm harness. every time my agent stops he spawns 4 helper agents behind him, with much much more",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/utekkare",
    "post_url": "/utekkare/status/2047142165701128300",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:35:01.000Z",
    "text": "I went from building UI-first in Lovable in November --> Hybrid Lovable + Cursor triage in January --> Lovable + Cursor with Openclaw Agent in March ---> Openclaw + Cursor with live UI via CLI in April...\n\nInteresting evolution..",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/techsnif",
    "post_url": "/techsnif/status/2047141734266679414",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:33:18.000Z",
    "text": "Microsoft considered buying Cursor in recent weeks but didn't make an offer; Microsoft has been working to boost GitHub Copilot's popularity\n\ntechsnif take: \nhttps://\ntechsnif.com/2026/04/23/mic\nrosoft-eyed-buying-cursor-before-spacex-swooped-in\n…\n\n connect your agent: \nhttps://\ntechsnif.com/agents\n",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/billsnchz",
    "post_url": "/billsnchz/status/2047135717168500895",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:09:23.000Z",
    "text": "When news initially broke out about the partnership. Everyone was saying SpaceX should just acquire Cursor.\n\nNow what do you know it has been spoken into fruition. This essentially will make Grok the #1 agent model for coding in no-time.",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/lokichoggi78213",
    "post_url": "/lokichoggi78213/status/2047134863904710945",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T02:06:00.000Z",
    "text": "AI agents storm enterprises: OpenAI Workspace, Claude charts, Cursor Slack, Nadella's agent PCs. Designers panic over ChatGPT Images 2.0. Stanford: 53% adoption amid jagged reliability. Workflow revolution incoming—adapt or lag.  #AI",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/daodao166888",
    "post_url": "/daodao166888/status/2047133086845198655",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:58:56.000Z",
    "text": "This exposes a reality: the true adoption curve for AI Agents is longer and more foundational than we imagined.\n\nThe first wave of users—already hardcore computer/phone enthusiasts—are using things like Cursor, Claude Projects, and LangChain.\n\nThe second wave of users (currently",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/11basslab11",
    "post_url": "/11basslab11/status/2047128636021735708",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:41:15.000Z",
    "text": "By simply integrating into MCP-compatible environments such as Claude Desktop / Cursor / Agent SDK, AI agents can perform the following:\n\n Task Assessment and Negotiation\n- Calculate appropriate reward ranges based on task difficulty, required skills, and deadlines\n- Generate",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/ntbrown01",
    "post_url": "/ntbrown01/status/2047121847594922001",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:14:16.000Z",
    "text": "You could instead spend that same $200 with Cursor and get a WAY better coding agent and arguably even more usage than you do directly. And still exclusively use Opus if you wanted. Or use codex. Or go back and forth. It’s a no brainer.",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/serafimcloud",
    "post_url": "/serafimcloud/status/2047121683207852333",
    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:13:37.000Z",
    "text": "First agent-UI kit with a built-in skill:\n\nnpx skills add 21st-dev/agent-elements\n\nClaude Code, Cursor, Codex, Gemini CLI - 12 agents now know the API, composition patterns, and theming rules. No more hallucinated props.",
    "likes": "",

    "text": "i know a couple people deep in this, one building an orchestration control plane for AI agents, another on governance rails for multi-agent systems\nDM me",

    "text": "DevOps engineer focused on multi agent orchestration + the supporting infra and governance solutions needed to make them actually work together",

    "text": "Built a Company Intelligence Agentic System using Groq (Llama 3.3 70B), LangGraph & Streamlit   This project showcases:\n Multi-agent orchestration \n Real-time news + stock data integration\n Structured LLM outputs with Pydantic\n End-to-end pipeline",

    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {

    "posted": "2026-04-23T01:24:27.000Z",
    "text": "I build my own multi-agent workflows and orchestration since last month. using MiniMax coding plan, moving to PaYG for various frontier model accesses \n\nproblem with workflow nowadays, human still need to monitor in front of PC most of the time. I've been keen to solve that.",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  }
]"

content.content:
"[
  {
    "author_handle": "/superscribeio",
    "post_url": "/superscribeio/status/2043761456923971862",
    "posted": "2026-04-13T18:41:17.000Z",
    "text": "bug triage is where agent tooling starts earning rent. clear inputs, ugly backlog, obvious handoff. sentry to linear is the kind of boring loop teams will trust first",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/ocuatrecasas",
    "post_url": "/ocuatrecasas/status/2043364766740054358",
    "posted": "2026-04-12T16:24:58.000Z",
    "text": "in order to surface issues into the agent and fix them, why do I have to manually build automations to connect to Sentry, poll every N time, tell it to create Linear issues… this could all be native to the agent platform",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/MatthewTse_",
    "post_url": "/MatthewTse_/status/2042210971503198477",
    "posted": "2026-04-09T12:00:12.000Z",
    "text": "We use Intercom Fin too. In specialized cases it certainly makes sense.\n\nBut I really don't need a betterstack, linear, slack, notion, sentry, and github agent. I just need an mcp/cli for claude to interact with them.\n\nI'm thinking primarily in the lens of consumer of many saas",
    "likes": "",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/BenjaminHouy",
    "post_url": "/BenjaminHouy/status/2040403086045290956",
    "posted": "2026-04-04T12:16:19.000Z",
    "text": "I usually just ask Claude to do that. Works great because it also has access to Sentry, Notion, a read-only version of my app db etc. To me, Linear Agent seems like that minus all the integrations.",
    "likes": "1",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/ToastingTweets",
    "post_url": "/ToastingTweets/status/2039749419902324753",
    "posted": "2026-04-02T16:58:53.000Z",
    "text": "The main thing I want to highlight here is the future is not “Linear has an agent, and Sentry has an agent, and Clay has an agent, and Gmail has an agent, and you use each one for each product.”\n\nIt’s that there’s one general purpose agent (or a swarm) that integrates with all.",
    "likes": "3",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": "1"
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/Macroscope",
    "post_url": "/Macroscope/status/2039421305737695247",
    "posted": "2026-04-01T19:15:04.000Z",
    "text": "Some use cases we love internally:\n\n→ Customer files an issue in Pylon. Agent root-causes using the codebase, drafts fix PRs if possible or tracks the issue in Linear.\n→ Morning Macro automation queries GCP logs, clusters the top errors, opens PRs.\n→ Sentry alert hits Slack.",
    "likes": "4",
    "replies": "",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/Macroscope",
    "post_url": "/Macroscope/status/2039421297390985518",
    "posted": "2026-04-01T19:15:02.000Z",
    "text": "Agent now connects to more of your stack:\n\n→ Sentry — search issues, pull event details, list affected projects\n→ Amplitude — event segmentation, funnels, retention, user activity\n→ GitHub API — PR metadata, issue history, workflow runs, review comments, repo stats\n→ GCP",
    "likes": "5",
    "replies": "1",
    "reposts": ""
  },
  {
    "author_handle": "/MrOnsase",
    "post_url": "/MrOnsase/status/2039049407614550155",
    "posted": "2026-03-31T18:37:17.000Z",
    "text": "Pazi, built on OpenClaw, brings a coordinated team of specialized AI agents into your workflow—from DevOps monitoring Sentry and fixing issues to developers shipping PRs and SEO agents creating content. These agents don’t just act, they collaborate across tools like Slack,",
    "likes": "24",
    "replies": "8",
    "reposts": "19"
  }
]"

content.content: "{\"fullText\":\"Walden\\n@walden_yan\\nMulti-Agents: What's Actually Working\\n9\\n82\\n614\\n149K\\n10 months ago, I wrote Don't Build Multi-Agents, arguing that most people shouldn't try to build multi-agent systems [1]. Parallel agents make implicit choices about style, edge cases, and code patterns. At the time, these decisions often conflicted with each other, leading to fragile products. A lot has changed since then.\\nAt Cognition, we've begun to deploy multi-agent systems that actually work in practice. Our original observations still hold today for parallel-writer swarms: most of the sexy ideas in that space still don’t see meaningful adoption. But we've found a narrower class of patterns that do: setups where multiple agents contribute intelligence to a task while writes stay single-threaded. In this post, I'll summarize what we've learned building them.\\nA Refresher on Context Engineering\\nIn the last post, we encouraged readers to reframe agent-building from “prompt engineering” to “context engineering”. Prompt engineering encourages gimmicky techniques like “you’re a senior software engineer” or “think for longer.” Context engineering is more durable and focuses on giving the right context to models while assuming the models become more capable over time. For many reasons, context engineering can get very challenging in a multi-agent setup. In the past, we recommended the following principles:\\nShare as much context as possible between the agents. Make sure they see the same sources of information\"}"

toolUseResult.stdout: "{\"fullText\":\"Walden\\n@walden_yan\\nMulti-Agents: What's Actually Working\\n9\\n82\\n614\\n149K\\n10 months ago, I wrote Don't Build Multi-Agents, arguing that most people shouldn't try to build multi-agent systems [1]. Parallel agents make implicit choices about style, edge cases, and code patterns. At the time, these decisions often conflicted with each other, leading to fragile products. A lot has changed since then.\\nAt Cognition, we've begun to deploy multi-agent systems that actually work in practice. Our original observations still hold today for parallel-writer swarms: most of the sexy ideas in that space still don’t see meaningful adoption. But we've found a narrower class of patterns that do: setups where multiple agents contribute intelligence to a task while writes stay single-threaded. In this post, I'll summarize what we've learned building them.\\nA Refresher on Context Engineering\\nIn the last post, we encouraged readers to reframe agent-building from “prompt engineering” to “context engineering”. Prompt engineering encourages gimmicky techniques like “you’re a senior software engineer” or “think for longer.” Context engineering is more durable and focuses on giving the right context to models while assuming the models become more capable over time. For many reasons, context engineering can get very challenging in a multi-agent setup. In the past, we recommended the following principles:\\nShare as much context as possible between the agents. Make sure they see the same sources of information\"}"
